---
title: "Forecasting Ohio Overdose Vineyards"
authors: "Nick Bermingham, David White, Nathan Willey"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
    code-tools: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    number-sections: true
    embed-resources: true
jupyter: python3
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

## Introduction

This notebook goes with our vineyards paper. We think of vineyards as one approach to the intersection of topological data analysis and time series analysis. One of the most important applications of time series analysis is forecasting: using the past to say something disciplined about the future. In this file we investigate what a forecasting analysis can and cannot say about the Ohio county-month overdose data.

We assume the reader has already read the uniformity, spatiotemporal, and confidence interval files. We remind the reader that our setting is the overdose-deaths-per-capita scalar field on the fixed Ohio county complex.

The randomness in this notebook is the same as the randomness in the uniformity file. In any given (county, month) pair, the number of overdose deaths could have been slightly different according to random events, like the exact dosage a person took, or whether a bystander was nearby with lifesaving naloxone. So when we make 95% forecast intervals for the future, it means we think that for 95% of these hypothetical worlds, their values would be in our intervals.

There are several natural forecasting questions.

First, which counties are forecasted to have unusually high overdose deaths per capita in future months? An answer to this would be extremely helpful to local health departments seeking to save lives, for resource allocation purposes. But this question is very difficult to answer.

Second, what will be the future statewide number of overdose deaths? This is easier to answer, but less useful for resource allocation because it ignores county-to-county differences. Even this forecasting problem is difficult if one only uses the history of the overdose time series. Normally, practitioners include many covariates, but doing so would take us too far afield.

Third, what will be the future of the vines in the vineyard? The Deep Research report and citation map used while drafting this section did not find prior work forecasting future overdose topology using vineyards. The closest related work forecasts overdose outcomes without TDA, or uses TDA for forecasting outside this vineyard-overdose setting. We show below that this forecasting task is difficult, but potentially useful: if maximum $H_1$ persistence could be forecast together with the location of the relevant feature, then the forecast would say something about the magnitude of future local spatial contrasts. We focus on the maximum finite $H_1$ persistence in the monthly sublevel set filtration, but the same scalar forecasting code could be applied to the second largest $H_1$ persistence and other ranked summaries.

In the current data, however, a simple forecastable county count model is not yet trustworthy enough for that role. It fails to achieve independent residuals so we do not trust it for forecasting reasons.

This motivates a narrower forecasting analysis. We forecast two scalar time series: statewide overdose deaths per capita, and maximum $H_1$ persistence, $M_t$. The statewide series is epidemiologically important but ignores differences among counties. The $M_t$ series is still a single time series, but it is obtained only after passing each monthly county surface through the Ohio adjacency complex, so it retains one kind of spatial/topological information. Forecasts of $M_t$ tell us about the magnitude of future spikes but don't tell us where they might happen.

Hence, separately, we attempt to forecast the county label associated with the strongest future $H_1$ feature. We do so using a categorical time-series forecast over county labels associated with the strongest monthly $H_1$ feature. This analysis can output either a top county or (as we use it) a list of the top 5 counties by predicted probability for that topological label.

## Related Work

The overdose forecasting literature is broad, but most of it is not topological. Ohio-specific work has used spatial and spatiotemporal statistical models to estimate latent opioid burden and county-level misuse prevalence [8, 12]. Ma, Tran, and White studied predictive relationships between Ohio drug seizures and overdose deaths using time-series and regression methods [20]. These papers are important context for us because they show that Ohio overdose mortality has temporal structure, spatial structure, and external drivers that a forecasting model should eventually use.

A separate group of overdose papers is closer to forecasting: CASTNet forecasts opioid overdose using community-attentive spatiotemporal neural networks [6], while [2] forecasts future opioid-incidence heat maps from past maps, [13] uses point-process models to predict overdose death hotspots from heterogeneous emergency medical services (EMS) and coroner data, [16] uses machine learning to forecast an early-warning system for emerging overdose patterns, and [15] studies monthly opioid-incident forecasting for rapid response in Kentucky. These papers forecast overdose burden or hotspot risk, but they do not compute persistent homology of the future overdose surface.

There is also a growing literature in which topological data analysis helps with prediction or forecasting, e.g., for epidemic modeling of Zika [14, 18] or for financial crashes [7]. In these examples, topology is usually a feature used by a downstream model, not the future object being forecast.

Vineyards have been used to track spatial and spatiotemporal anomalies in COVID-19 data [9]. Various time-dependent topological summaries, including persistence-vineyard information, has been used to predict future behavior in tumor-immune simulations [21]. These works show that dynamic topology can be tracked and, in some settings, used predictively. 

Our analysis sits between these traditions. We fit traditional time series models (including SARIMA models, explained below), use residual diagnostics to check that the models leave independent residuals, use classical forecasting from time-series analysis [11, 17], and use residual bootstrap intervals when we want empirical prediction bands. We compute topology on the same fixed Ohio county complex used elsewhere in this project. Stability of persistence diagrams under perturbations of scalar fields [4] is the reason it is meaningful to compare monthly diagrams and forecasted perturbations of the deaths-per-capita field.

## Roadmap to this file

This file has five main parts. First, it reads the county-month data, defines the fixed Ohio county complex, and reads the historical $H_1$ summaries. Second, it attempts to forecast (county, month) overdose death rates, explains time series forecasting, and constructs models to forecast statewide overdose death rate and max $H_1$ persistence. Third, it reports several forecasts and their cross-validation diagnostics. Fourth, it studies categorical forecasts of the county label associated with the strongest $H_1$ feature. Finally, it extracts actionable public health insights from the forecasts.

## Setup code

This notebook renders quickly by reading saved outputs, but the source code that creates those outputs is visible below. Expensive chunks are folded and marked `eval: false`; running them regenerates the CSV, PNG, and PDF files used by the later display chunks. In the Python code, expressions such as `f"{prefix}_persistence"` are format strings: they build a column name by substituting the current value of `prefix`. The code below imports the modules we need and sets up the paths to the files we will read.

```{python}
#| label: forecast-setup
from __future__ import annotations

from pathlib import Path
import math
import warnings

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from IPython.display import Image, display

from statsmodels.tsa.statespace.sarimax import SARIMAX
from statsmodels.stats.diagnostic import acorr_ljungbox
from statsmodels.graphics.tsaplots import plot_acf

HERE = Path.cwd().resolve()
if (HERE / "forecasting-vineyards.qmd").exists():
    FORECAST_DIR = HERE
elif (HERE / "forecasting files" / "forecasting-vineyards.qmd").exists():
    FORECAST_DIR = HERE / "forecasting files"
else:
    raise FileNotFoundError("Could not locate forecasting-vineyards.qmd or forecasting files/.")

OUTPUT_DIR = FORECAST_DIR / "forecasting_vineyards_outputs"
FIGURE_DIR = OUTPUT_DIR / "figures"
OUTPUT_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
FIGURE_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
```

The saved-output production code is shown below in the sections where the outputs first appear. Those chunks are marked `eval: false` so ordinary rendering reads saved CSV, PNG, and PDF files instead of rerunning the expensive simulations and exact persistence calculations.

## Data and Preliminary Mortality Counts

The county-month data contain 88 Ohio counties and 213 observed months. The first month is January 2007 and the last month in the file is September 2024. The topology is always computed on the same fixed Ohio county complex; the scalar value attached to each county changes by month.

The final months of our data are incomplete because overdose deaths often require toxicology and death-investigation processing. In the snapshot used here, downloaded in October 2024, the statewide deaths-per-capita series drops implausibly sharply near the end. The Ohio Department of Health said at that time that data through 2022 was trustworthy but later data was still preliminary. The 2024 data was even more preliminary, as even at the state level it had not been fully reported yet, to say nothing of the death investigation or CDC national level work. Thus, these values are not used for model training or cross validation. The main validation periods are completed years: a 2022 holdout obtained by training through December 2021, and a 2023 holdout obtained by training through December 2022.

The following non-executed source chunks show how the historical $H_1$ summaries are produced. The first chunk defines the data-loading, topology, and summary helpers. The second chunk uses those helpers to load the county-month files, mark preliminary months, compute exact historical $H_1$ summaries, and save the data output files used below.

```{python}
#| label: forecasting-data-helper-source
#| eval: false
import contextlib
import io
import json
import math
import os
import sys
import warnings
from dataclasses import dataclass
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Any

os.environ.setdefault("MPLCONFIGDIR", "/tmp/matplotlib-cache")

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import statsmodels.api as sm
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf
from scipy import stats
from statsmodels.graphics.tsaplots import plot_acf
from statsmodels.nonparametric.smoothers_lowess import lowess
from statsmodels.tsa.seasonal import STL
from statsmodels.tsa.statespace.sarimax import SARIMAX
from statsmodels.tools.sm_exceptions import ConvergenceWarning

try:
    from tqdm.auto import tqdm
except Exception:
    def tqdm(x, **kwargs):
        """Return an iterable unchanged when tqdm is unavailable.

        Args:
            See the function signature for input names and types.

        Returns:
            The dataframe, series, array, model object, or scalar described by the function name.
        """
        return x

HERE = Path.cwd()
if not (HERE.parent / "data" / "county_drug_death.csv").exists() and (HERE / "forecasting files" / "county_drug_death.csv").exists():
    HERE = HERE / "forecasting files"

OUTPUT_DIR = HERE / "forecasting_vineyards_outputs"
FIGURE_DIR = OUTPUT_DIR / "figures"
OUTPUT_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
FIGURE_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

VINEYARD_CODE = HERE.parent
if str(VINEYARD_CODE) not in sys.path:
    sys.path.insert(0, str(VINEYARD_CODE))
from polygonal_surface import PolygonalSurface as PS

CONFIG = {
    "counts_wide_path": HERE.parent / "data" / "county_drug_death.csv",
    "rates_wide_path": HERE.parent / "data" / "pop_normalized_county_drug_death.csv",
    "population_wide_path": HERE.parent / "data" / "ohio_county_populations.csv",
    "adjacency_path": HERE.parent / "data" / "ohio_neighbors.txt",
    "bootstrap_significance_path": HERE / "forecasting_vineyards_outputs" / "ohio_monthly_h1_bootstrap_significance.csv",
    "random_seed": int(os.environ.get("FORECAST_REBUILD_RANDOM_SEED", "20260618")),
    "n_holdout_simulations": int(os.environ.get("FORECAST_REBUILD_HOLDOUT_SIMULATIONS", "120")),
    "n_future_simulations": int(os.environ.get("FORECAST_REBUILD_FUTURE_SIMULATIONS", "120")),
    "top_rank_count": int(os.environ.get("FORECAST_REBUILD_TOP_RANK_COUNT", "10")),
    "future_horizon_months": int(os.environ.get("FORECAST_REBUILD_FUTURE_HORIZON", "12")),
    "completed_training_end": pd.Timestamp("2023-12-01"),
    "preliminary_start": pd.Timestamp("2024-01-01"),
    "holdouts": [
        {"experiment": "C", "train_end": pd.Timestamp("2020-12-01"), "holdout_start": pd.Timestamp("2021-01-01"), "holdout_end": pd.Timestamp("2021-12-01")},
        {"experiment": "A", "train_end": pd.Timestamp("2021-12-01"), "holdout_start": pd.Timestamp("2022-01-01"), "holdout_end": pd.Timestamp("2022-12-01")},
        {"experiment": "B", "train_end": pd.Timestamp("2022-12-01"), "holdout_start": pd.Timestamp("2023-01-01"), "holdout_end": pd.Timestamp("2023-12-01")},
    ],
}

rng_global = np.random.default_rng(CONFIG["random_seed"])
print(json.dumps({k: str(v) for k, v in CONFIG.items() if not isinstance(v, list)}, indent=2))

# ---- Data-loading helpers: normalize county labels, reshape monthly files, load population, and validate the county-month grid ----

@dataclass
class CountModelFit:
    result: Any
    alpha: float
    formula: str
    method: str
    converged: bool
    n_train_rows: int
    n_train_months: int
    train_start: pd.Timestamp
    train_end: pd.Timestamp


def parse_month_label(label: str) -> tuple[int, int]:
    """Parse a wide monthly column label such as 2007_1.

    Args:
        See the function signature for input names and types.

    Returns:
        The dataframe, series, array, model object, or scalar described by the function name.
    """
    year_text, month_text = str(label).split("_")
    return int(year_text), int(month_text)


def canonical_county_name(series: pd.Series) -> pd.Series:
    """Normalize county labels so data files match the Ohio complex.

    Args:
        See the function signature for input names and types.

    Returns:
        The dataframe, series, array, model object, or scalar described by the function name.
    """
    return (
        series.astype(str)
        .str.replace(r"\s+County,\s*OH$", "", regex=True)
        .str.replace(r"\s+County$", "", regex=True)
        .str.strip()
        .replace({"Van Wert": "VanWert"})
    )


def wide_county_month_to_long(path: Path, value_name: str) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Reshape a county-by-month wide file into a long county-month table.

    Args:
        See the function signature for input names and types.

    Returns:
        The dataframe, series, array, model object, or scalar described by the function name.
    """
    wide = pd.read_csv(path).rename(columns={"Unnamed: 0": "county", "": "county"})
    if "county" not in wide.columns:
        wide = wide.rename(columns={wide.columns[0]: "county"})
    wide["county"] = canonical_county_name(wide["county"])
    long = wide.melt(id_vars="county", var_name="month_label", value_name=value_name)
    parsed = long["month_label"].map(parse_month_label)
    long["year"] = [x[0] for x in parsed]
    long["month"] = [x[1] for x in parsed]
    long[value_name] = pd.to_numeric(long[value_name], errors="coerce")
    long["date"] = pd.to_datetime(dict(year=long["year"], month=long["month"], day=1))
    return long


def load_population_by_county_year(path: Path) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Load annual county populations and reshape them to county-year rows.

    Args:
        See the function signature for input names and types.

    Returns:
        The dataframe, series, array, model object, or scalar described by the function name.
    """
    pop = pd.read_csv(path).rename(columns={"Unnamed: 0": "county", "": "county"})
    if "county" not in pop.columns:
        pop = pop.rename(columns={pop.columns[0]: "county"})
    pop["county"] = canonical_county_name(pop["county"])
    year_cols = [c for c in pop.columns if str(c).isdigit()]
    pop_long = pop.melt(id_vars="county", value_vars=year_cols, var_name="year", value_name="population")
    pop_long["year"] = pop_long["year"].astype(int)
    pop_long["population"] = pd.to_numeric(pop_long["population"], errors="coerce")
    return pop_long


def read_surface(adjacency_path: Path) -> Any:
    """Load the fixed Ohio county adjacency complex.

    Args:
        See the function signature for input names and types.

    Returns:
        The fixed Ohio polygonal surface object.
    """
    with contextlib.redirect_stdout(io.StringIO()):
        return PS.read_adj(str(adjacency_path))


def load_county_month_data(config: dict[str, Any], county_order: list[str]) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Load, merge, validate, and sort the county-month overdose data.

    Args:
        See the function signature for input names and types.

    Returns:
        The dataframe, series, array, model object, or scalar described by the function name.
    """
    counts = wide_county_month_to_long(Path(config["counts_wide_path"]), "deaths")
    rates = wide_county_month_to_long(Path(config["rates_wide_path"]), "deaths_per_capita_file")
    pop = load_population_by_county_year(Path(config["population_wide_path"]))

    df = counts.merge(
        rates[["county", "date", "deaths_per_capita_file"]],
        on=["county", "date"],
        how="inner",
        validate="one_to_one",
    )
    df = df[df["county"].isin(county_order)].copy()
    df = df.merge(pop, on=["county", "year"], how="left", validate="many_to_one")

    positive = (df["deaths"] > 0) & (df["deaths_per_capita_file"] > 0)
    df["population_from_rate"] = np.nan
    df.loc[positive, "population_from_rate"] = df.loc[positive, "deaths"] / df.loc[positive, "deaths_per_capita_file"]
    df["population"] = df["population"].fillna(df["population_from_rate"])
    df = df.sort_values(["county", "date"]).reset_index(drop=True)
    df["population"] = df.groupby("county")["population"].transform(lambda s: s.ffill().bfill())
    df["population"] = df.groupby("county")["population"].transform(lambda s: s.fillna(s.median()))
    df["population"] = df["population"].round().astype(int)
    df["deaths_per_capita"] = df["deaths"] / df["population"].astype(float)
    df["month_label"] = df["year"].astype(str) + "_" + df["month"].astype(str)
    date_order = {d: i for i, d in enumerate(sorted(df["date"].unique()))}
    df["time_index"] = df["date"].map(date_order).astype(int)
    df = df.sort_values(["county", "date"]).reset_index(drop=True)

    required = ["county", "year", "month", "date", "population", "deaths", "deaths_per_capita"]
    if df[required].isna().any().any():
        raise ValueError("Missing required county-month data:\n" + str(df[required].isna().sum()))
    if df["county"].nunique() != 88:
        raise ValueError(f"Expected 88 counties; found {df['county'].nunique()}.")
    expected_rows = df["county"].nunique() * df["date"].nunique()
    if len(df) != expected_rows:
        raise ValueError(f"Incomplete county-month grid: {len(df)} rows, expected {expected_rows}.")
    if (df["population"] <= 0).any() or (df["deaths"] < 0).any() or (df["deaths_per_capita"] < 0).any():
        raise ValueError("Population, deaths, and rates failed non-negativity checks.")
    return df


def statewide_series(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Aggregate county rows into a statewide monthly deaths-per-capita series.

    Args:
        See the function signature for input names and types.

    Returns:
        The dataframe, series, array, model object, or scalar described by the function name.
    """
    return (
        df.groupby("date", as_index=False)
        .agg(statewide_deaths=("deaths", "sum"), statewide_population=("population", "sum"))
        .assign(statewide_rate=lambda x: x["statewide_deaths"] / x["statewide_population"])
        .sort_values("date")
    )
```

The following code defines several helper functions, including error metrics, interval summaries, threshold choices, and simple benchmark forecasts to compare our more sophisticated forecasts to.

```{python}
#| label: forecasting-metric-helper-source
#| eval: false
def rmse(observed: pd.Series, predicted: pd.Series) -> float:
    """Compute root mean squared error.

    Args:
        See the function signature for input names and types.

    Returns:
        The dataframe, series, array, model object, or scalar described by the function name.
    """
    return float(np.sqrt(np.mean((np.asarray(observed, dtype=float) - np.asarray(predicted, dtype=float)) ** 2)))


def mae(observed: pd.Series, predicted: pd.Series) -> float:
    """Compute mean absolute error.

    Args:
        See the function signature for input names and types.

    Returns:
        The dataframe, series, array, model object, or scalar described by the function name.
    """
    return float(np.mean(np.abs(np.asarray(observed, dtype=float) - np.asarray(predicted, dtype=float))))


def interval_summary(values: pd.Series, prefix: str) -> pd.Series:
    """Summarize a simulated distribution with central values and quantile intervals.

    Args:
        See the function signature for input names and types.

    Returns:
        The dataframe, series, array, model object, or scalar described by the function name.
    """
    x = np.asarray(values, dtype=float)
    return pd.Series({
        f"{prefix}_mean": float(np.mean(x)),
        f"{prefix}_median": float(np.median(x)),
        f"{prefix}_lower_50": float(np.quantile(x, 0.25)),
        f"{prefix}_upper_50": float(np.quantile(x, 0.75)),
        f"{prefix}_lower_80": float(np.quantile(x, 0.10)),
        f"{prefix}_upper_80": float(np.quantile(x, 0.90)),
        f"{prefix}_lower_95": float(np.quantile(x, 0.025)),
        f"{prefix}_upper_95": float(np.quantile(x, 0.975)),
    })


def choose_hotspot_threshold(train_df: pd.DataFrame) -> float:
    """Choose the county-rate exceedance threshold from training data.

    Args:
        See the function signature for input names and types.

    Returns:
        The dataframe, series, array, model object, or scalar described by the function name.
    """
    return float(train_df["deaths_per_capita"].quantile(0.90))


def choose_h1_threshold(observed_h1: pd.DataFrame) -> float:
    """Choose the reference max-H1 persistence threshold.

    Args:
        See the function signature for input names and types.

    Returns:
        The dataframe, series, array, model object, or scalar described by the function name.
    """
    sig_path = Path(CONFIG["bootstrap_significance_path"])
    if sig_path.exists():
        sig = pd.read_csv(sig_path)
        if "band" in sig.columns and sig["band"].notna().any():
            return float(sig["band"].max())
    return float(observed_h1["max_h1_persistence"].quantile(0.90))


def normal_tail_probability(threshold: float, mean: float, se: float) -> float:
    """Compute a Gaussian upper-tail probability for a forecast threshold.

    Args:
        See the function signature for input names and types.

    Returns:
        The dataframe, series, array, model object, or scalar described by the function name.
    """
    if not np.isfinite(se) or se <= 0:
        return float(mean > threshold)
    z = (threshold - mean) / se
    return float(0.5 * math.erfc(z / math.sqrt(2.0)))


def baseline_statewide_forecasts(train_state: pd.DataFrame, holdout_dates: list[pd.Timestamp]) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Compute simple statewide-rate benchmark forecasts.

    Args:
        See the function signature for input names and types.

    Returns:
        The dataframe, series, array, model object, or scalar described by the function name.
    """
    train = train_state.set_index("date").sort_index()
    rows = []
    last_rate = float(train["statewide_rate"].iloc[-1])
    recent_mean = float(train["statewide_rate"].tail(12).mean())
    for horizon, date in enumerate(holdout_dates, start=1):
        same_month_history = train[train.index.month == date.month]["statewide_rate"]
        last_year_date = date - pd.DateOffset(years=1)
        seasonal_naive = float(train.loc[last_year_date, "statewide_rate"]) if last_year_date in train.index else float(same_month_history.iloc[-1])
        seasonal_mean = float(same_month_history.mean())
        rows.extend([
            {"model": "seasonal_naive", "horizon": horizon, "date": date, "predicted_rate": seasonal_naive, "lower_80": np.nan, "upper_80": np.nan, "lower_95": np.nan, "upper_95": np.nan},
            {"model": "last_observation", "horizon": horizon, "date": date, "predicted_rate": last_rate, "lower_80": np.nan, "upper_80": np.nan, "lower_95": np.nan, "upper_95": np.nan},
            {"model": "recent_12_month_average", "horizon": horizon, "date": date, "predicted_rate": recent_mean, "lower_80": np.nan, "upper_80": np.nan, "lower_95": np.nan, "upper_95": np.nan},
            {"model": "same_month_training_mean", "horizon": horizon, "date": date, "predicted_rate": seasonal_mean, "lower_80": np.nan, "upper_80": np.nan, "lower_95": np.nan, "upper_95": np.nan},
        ])
    return pd.DataFrame(rows)



def h1_diagram_sublevel(ps: Any, county_order: list[str], values: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    """Compute the exact finite H1 Sublevel set diagram for one county-rate surface.

    Args:
        See the function signature for input names and types.

    Returns:
        The dataframe, series, array, model object, or scalar described by the function name.
    """
    region_values = {county: float(value) for county, value in zip(county_order, values)}
    with contextlib.redirect_stdout(io.StringIO()):
        st = ps.sublevel_SC(region_values)
    st.compute_persistence(homology_coeff_field=2, min_persistence=0.0)
    intervals = np.asarray(st.persistence_intervals_in_dimension(1), dtype=float)
    if intervals.size == 0:
        return np.empty((0, 2), dtype=float)
    intervals = intervals[np.isfinite(intervals).all(axis=1)]
    return intervals if intervals.size else np.empty((0, 2), dtype=float)


def ranked_h1_rows(diagram: np.ndarray, rank_count: int, base: dict[str, Any]) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:
    """Convert one H1 diagram into ranked persistence rows.

    Args:
        See the function signature for input names and types.

    Returns:
        The dataframe, series, array, model object, or scalar described by the function name.
    """
    if len(diagram):
        pers = diagram[:, 1] - diagram[:, 0]
        order = np.argsort(-pers)
        sorted_diagram = diagram[order]
        sorted_pers = pers[order]
    else:
        sorted_diagram = np.empty((0, 2), dtype=float)
        sorted_pers = np.array([], dtype=float)
    rows = []
    for rank in range(1, rank_count + 1):
        row = dict(base)
        row["rank"] = rank
        row["h1_point_count"] = int(len(diagram))
        if rank <= len(sorted_pers):
            row["birth"] = float(sorted_diagram[rank - 1, 0])
            row["death"] = float(sorted_diagram[rank - 1, 1])
            row["persistence"] = float(sorted_pers[rank - 1])
        else:
            row["birth"] = np.nan
            row["death"] = np.nan
            row["persistence"] = 0.0
        rows.append(row)
    return rows


def compute_observed_h1(df: pd.DataFrame, ps: Any, county_order: list[str], rank_count: int) -> tuple[pd.DataFrame, pd.DataFrame]:
    """Compute exact observed monthly H1 summaries and ranked features.

    Args:
        See the function signature for input names and types.

    Returns:
        The dataframe, series, array, model object, or scalar described by the function name.
    """
    matrix = df.pivot(index="county", columns="date", values="deaths_per_capita").loc[county_order]
    summary_rows = []
    ranked_rows = []
    for month_index, date in enumerate(tqdm(list(matrix.columns), desc="Observed exact H1")):
        values = matrix[date].to_numpy(dtype=float)
        diagram = h1_diagram_sublevel(ps, county_order, values)
        pers = diagram[:, 1] - diagram[:, 0] if len(diagram) else np.array([], dtype=float)
        base = {"date": date, "month_label": f"{date.year}_{date.month}", "month_index": month_index, "year": date.year, "month": date.month, "source": "observed"}
        summary_rows.append({**base, "h1_point_count": int(len(diagram)), "max_h1_persistence": float(pers.max()) if len(pers) else 0.0, "top_ranked_total_persistence": float(np.sort(pers)[-rank_count:].sum()) if len(pers) else 0.0})
        ranked_rows.extend(ranked_h1_rows(diagram, rank_count, base))
    return pd.DataFrame(summary_rows), pd.DataFrame(ranked_rows)


def compute_simulated_h1(simulations: pd.DataFrame, ps: Any, county_order: list[str], rank_count: int, label: str) -> tuple[pd.DataFrame, pd.DataFrame, pd.DataFrame]:
    """Compute exact H1 summaries for simulated county-rate surfaces.

    Args:
        See the function signature for input names and types.

    Returns:
        The dataframe, series, array, model object, or scalar described by the function name.
    """
    matrix = simulations.pivot_table(index=["simulation", "horizon", "date"], columns="county", values="deaths_per_capita", aggfunc="first").sort_index()
    summary_rows = []
    ranked_rows = []
    failures = []
    for (simulation, horizon, date), row in tqdm(matrix.iterrows(), total=len(matrix), desc=f"{label} exact H1"):
        try:
            values = row.loc[county_order].to_numpy(dtype=float)
            diagram = h1_diagram_sublevel(ps, county_order, values)
            pers = diagram[:, 1] - diagram[:, 0] if len(diagram) else np.array([], dtype=float)
            base = {"source": label, "simulation": int(simulation), "horizon": int(horizon), "date": date}
            summary_rows.append({**base, "h1_point_count": int(len(diagram)), "max_h1_persistence": float(pers.max()) if len(pers) else 0.0, "top_ranked_total_persistence": float(np.sort(pers)[-rank_count:].sum()) if len(pers) else 0.0})
            ranked_rows.extend(ranked_h1_rows(diagram, rank_count, base))
        except Exception as exc:
            failures.append({"source": label, "simulation": int(simulation), "horizon": int(horizon), "date": date, "error": repr(exc)})
    failures_df = pd.DataFrame(failures)
    failure_rate = len(failures_df) / max(len(matrix), 1)
    if failure_rate > 0.10:
        warnings.warn(f"More than 10% of {label} H1 computations failed: {failure_rate:.1%}")
    return pd.DataFrame(summary_rows), pd.DataFrame(ranked_rows), failures_df
```

The following cell is for data preparation: we load the Ohio county data, split completed/preliminary months, and record the $H_1$ persistence information as in our paper.

```{python}
#| label: forecasting-data-and-historical-h1-source
#| eval: false


ps = read_surface(Path(CONFIG["adjacency_path"]))
county_order = [name for name in ps.R.keys() if not str(name).startswith("exterior")]
df_all = load_county_month_data(CONFIG, county_order)
state_all = statewide_series(df_all)

completed_df = df_all[df_all["date"] <= CONFIG["completed_training_end"]].copy()
preliminary_df = df_all[df_all["date"] >= CONFIG["preliminary_start"]].copy()
state_completed = statewide_series(completed_df)

snapshot_summary = pd.DataFrame([
    {"quantity": "county_count", "value": df_all["county"].nunique()},
    {"quantity": "observed_month_count", "value": df_all["date"].nunique()},
    {"quantity": "first_observed_month", "value": df_all["date"].min().date().isoformat()},
    {"quantity": "last_observed_month", "value": df_all["date"].max().date().isoformat()},
    {"quantity": "completed_training_end", "value": CONFIG["completed_training_end"].date().isoformat()},
    {"quantity": "preliminary_months_excluded_from_main_training", "value": preliminary_df["date"].nunique()},
    {"quantity": "missing_population_rows", "value": int(df_all["population"].isna().sum())},
])
snapshot_summary.to_csv(OUTPUT_DIR / "forecast_data_snapshot_summary.csv", index=False)
snapshot_summary

# ---- Preliminary-tail figure: mark the late-2024 months excluded from main forecast training ----

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 4.6))
ax.plot(state_all["date"], state_all["statewide_rate"], color="#2f3e46", lw=1.5, label="Observed statewide rate")
ax.axvspan(CONFIG["preliminary_start"], state_all["date"].max(), color="#d62828", alpha=0.12, label="Preliminary tail excluded from main fitting")
ax.set_title("Statewide overdose deaths per capita, with preliminary 2024 tail marked")
ax.set_xlabel("Month")
ax.set_ylabel("Deaths per capita")
ax.legend()
fig.tight_layout()
for ext in ["png", "pdf"]:
    fig.savefig(FIGURE_DIR / f"statewide_rate_preliminary_tail.{ext}", dpi=180 if ext == "png" else None, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()

# ---- Historical topology: compute observed exact monthly H1 diagrams and ranked H1 summaries ----

observed_h1, observed_ranked_h1 = compute_observed_h1(completed_df, ps, county_order, CONFIG["top_rank_count"])
observed_h1.to_csv(OUTPUT_DIR / "historical_h1_persistence_summary.csv", index=False)
observed_ranked_h1.to_csv(OUTPUT_DIR / "historical_h1_ranked_persistence.csv", index=False)
h1_threshold = choose_h1_threshold(observed_h1)
observed_h1.tail()

# ---- Historical summary plots: show statewide deaths, max county rate, and max H1 persistence through 2023 ----

hist_state = statewide_series(completed_df)
max_county_rate = completed_df.groupby("date", as_index=False).agg(max_county_rate=("deaths_per_capita", "max"))
fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 1, figsize=(11, 9), sharex=True)
axes[0].plot(observed_h1["date"], observed_h1["max_h1_persistence"], color="#264653")
axes[0].axhline(h1_threshold, color="#a23b72", ls=":", lw=1.2, label="Reference H1 threshold")
axes[0].set_ylabel("Max H1 persistence")
axes[0].legend()
axes[1].plot(hist_state["date"], hist_state["statewide_deaths"], color="#457b9d")
axes[1].set_ylabel("Statewide deaths")
axes[2].plot(max_county_rate["date"], max_county_rate["max_county_rate"], color="#2a9d8f")
axes[2].set_ylabel("Max county rate")
axes[2].set_xlabel("Month")
fig.suptitle("Historical count, rate, and H1 summaries through December 2023", y=0.995)
fig.tight_layout()
for ext in ["png", "pdf"]:
    fig.savefig(FIGURE_DIR / f"historical_h1_statewide_rate_timeseries.{ext}", dpi=180 if ext == "png" else None, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()
```

After the code chunks above have been run once, ordinary rendering uses the helper below to read the saved outputs in the sections where they are displayed. There is also a helper function to use to test our forecast models. 

```{python}
#| label: forecast-read-helpers
pd.set_option("display.max_columns", 40)
pd.set_option("display.width", 160)

def read_derived_csv(name: str, **kwargs) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Read one saved CSV created by the visible production code.

    Args:
        name: File name inside the forecasting output directory.
        **kwargs: Additional keyword arguments passed to pandas.read_csv.

    Returns:
        The requested CSV as a dataframe.
    """
    path = OUTPUT_DIR / name
    if not path.exists():
        raise FileNotFoundError(path)
    return pd.read_csv(path, **kwargs)


def show_table(df: pd.DataFrame, n: int = 12, columns: list[str] | None = None) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Return a short display version of a dataframe.

    Args:
        df: Source dataframe.
        n: Number of rows to keep.
        columns: Optional subset of columns to display.

    Returns:
        A dataframe suitable for compact display.
    """
    out = df.copy()
    if columns is not None:
        out = out[columns]
    return out.head(n)

def heldout_correlation_squared(observed: pd.Series, predicted: pd.Series) -> float:
    """Compute squared heldout Pearson correlation when it is defined.

    Args:
        observed: Observed values in the holdout window.
        predicted: Point forecasts for the same holdout window.

    Returns:
        The squared Pearson correlation, or NaN if either vector is constant or incomplete.
    """
    joined = pd.DataFrame({"observed": observed, "predicted": predicted}).dropna()
    if len(joined) < 2 or joined["observed"].std(ddof=0) == 0 or joined["predicted"].std(ddof=0) == 0:
        return np.nan
    return float(joined["observed"].corr(joined["predicted"]) ** 2)

```

## What are we forecasting?

Let $D(c,t)$ be the number of overdose deaths in county $c$ during month $t$, and let $N(c,t)$ be the county population denominator. The county rate is

$$
R(c,t) = \frac{D(c,t)}{N(c,t)}.
$$

A county hotspot forecast would estimate probabilities such as

$$
P\{R(c,T+h) > r_*\},
$$

where $r_*$ is a chosen high-rate threshold and $h$ is the forecast horizon. That is the most directly actionable target, but it requires a county-level forecasting model that gives reliable future county surfaces.

The topological scalar used here is

$$
M_t = \max_{(b,d) \in D_t^{(1)}} (d-b),
$$

where $D_t^{(1)}$ is the finite $H_1$ persistence diagram for the deaths-per-capita field in month $t$. If a month has no finite $H_1$ points, we set $M_t=0$. This is the maximum persistence among loop-like sublevel set features in that month.

In a sublevel set filtration, low-rate counties enter first and high-rate counties enter later. An $H_1$ feature appears when the counties that have already entered form a loop around a region that has not yet entered. The death value is the threshold where the hole fills in. Persistence is the gap between the birth and death thresholds. It is a rate-gap-like spatial contrast, but it is not a county forecast. Many different county-rate surfaces can have the same maximum $H_1$ persistence.

## Why the Simple County Forecast Is Not Used as the Main Forecast

The code in this section fits a forecastable negative-binomial model for county-month death counts. First, we describe the model. Let $D(c,t)$ be the overdose death count in county $c$ and month $t$, and let $N(c,t)$ be the county population. We fit

$$
D(c,t) \sim \operatorname{NegBin}(\mu(c,t), \alpha)
$$

with

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log \mu(c,t)
&= \log N(c,t) + \beta_c + \gamma_{m(t)} + \eta t \\
&\quad + \theta_1 \log(1 + D(c,t-1))
+ \theta_2 R(c,t-1)
+ \theta_3 \log(1 + D_{\mathrm{OH}}(t-1)).
\end{aligned}
$$

Here $\beta_c$ is a county baseline, $\gamma_{m(t)}$ is a month-of-year effect, $R(c,t-1)$ is the previous county deaths-per-capita value, and $D_{\mathrm{OH}}(t-1)$ is the previous statewide death count.

What makes the model "forecastable" is that $D(c,t+1)$ depends only on the past and present, not terms like $D(c',t+1)$ that would not be known at time $t$.


The next non-executed source chunks show the attempted county-level forecasting model. They fit the forecastable negative-binomial count model, simulate county-month death counts in completed-year holdouts, compute induced $H_1$ summaries, and save the county-model diagnostics. We provide the code because this failed attempt explains why the later forecasts focus on scalar statewide and scalar topological series, and because future readers may wish to expand on this model.

```{python}
#| label: county-negative-binomial-model-source
#| eval: false
# ---- Forecastable county count model: add lagged predictors, fit the negative-binomial GLM, and simulate future county counts recursively ----

def add_lag_features(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Add lagged county and statewide predictors used by the forecastable count model.

    Args:
        See the function signature for input names and types.

    Returns:
        The dataframe, series, array, model object, or scalar described by the function name.
    """
    work = df.sort_values(["county", "date"]).copy()
    work["lag_deaths"] = work.groupby("county")["deaths"].shift(1).fillna(0.0)
    work["lag_rate"] = work.groupby("county")["deaths_per_capita"].shift(1).fillna(0.0)
    statewide_lag = work.groupby("date")["deaths"].sum().sort_index().shift(1).rename("statewide_lag_deaths")
    work = work.merge(statewide_lag, left_on="date", right_index=True, how="left")
    work["statewide_lag_deaths"] = work["statewide_lag_deaths"].fillna(0.0)
    work["log1p_lag_deaths"] = np.log1p(work["lag_deaths"])
    work["lag_rate_filled"] = work["lag_rate"]
    work["log1p_statewide_lag_deaths"] = np.log1p(work["statewide_lag_deaths"])
    work["log_population"] = np.log(work["population"].astype(float))
    return work


def fit_count_model(train_df: pd.DataFrame) -> CountModelFit:
    """Fit the negative-binomial county-month count model used as a diagnostic attempt.

    Args:
        See the function signature for input names and types.

    Returns:
        A CountModelFit object containing the fitted model and metadata.
    """
    work = add_lag_features(train_df)
    formula = "deaths ~ C(county) + C(month) + time_index + log1p_lag_deaths + lag_rate_filled + log1p_statewide_lag_deaths"
    offset = work["log_population"]
    poisson = smf.glm(formula=formula, data=work, offset=offset, family=sm.families.Poisson()).fit(maxiter=200)
    mu_p = np.maximum(np.asarray(poisson.predict(work, offset=offset), dtype=float), 1e-9)
    pearson = np.sum((work["deaths"].to_numpy(dtype=float) - mu_p) ** 2 / mu_p)
    df_resid = max(int(poisson.df_resid), 1)
    alpha = max((pearson / df_resid - 1.0) / max(float(np.mean(mu_p)), 1e-9), 1e-8)
    nb_model = smf.glm(
        formula=formula,
        data=work,
        offset=offset,
        family=sm.families.NegativeBinomial(alpha=alpha),
    )
    with warnings.catch_warnings(record=True) as caught:
        warnings.simplefilter("always")
        nb_result = nb_model.fit(maxiter=200)
    converged = bool(getattr(nb_result, "converged", True)) and not any("convergence" in str(w.message).lower() for w in caught)
    return CountModelFit(
        result=nb_result,
        alpha=float(alpha),
        formula=formula,
        method="statsmodels GLM NegativeBinomial with Pearson alpha estimated from a Poisson fit",
        converged=converged,
        n_train_rows=len(work),
        n_train_months=work["date"].nunique(),
        train_start=work["date"].min(),
        train_end=work["date"].max(),
    )


def future_base_for_dates(template_df: pd.DataFrame, start_after: pd.Timestamp, horizon_count: int, observed_df_for_population: pd.DataFrame | None = None) -> dict[int, pd.DataFrame]:
    """Build county-month design rows for recursive future forecast months.

    Args:
        See the function signature for input names and types.

    Returns:
        The dataframe, series, array, model object, or scalar described by the function name.
    """
    last_time_index = int(template_df["time_index"].max())
    last_pop = template_df.sort_values("date").groupby("county", sort=False).tail(1)[["county", "population"]]
    observed_pop = None if observed_df_for_population is None else observed_df_for_population[["county", "date", "population"]].copy()
    bases = {}
    for horizon in range(1, horizon_count + 1):
        date = start_after + pd.DateOffset(months=horizon)
        base = last_pop.copy()
        if observed_pop is not None:
            pop_this = observed_pop.loc[observed_pop["date"].eq(date), ["county", "population"]]
            if len(pop_this):
                base = base.drop(columns=["population"]).merge(pop_this, on="county", how="left")
                base["population"] = base["population"].ffill().bfill()
        base["date"] = date
        base["year"] = date.year
        base["month"] = date.month
        base["month_label"] = f"{date.year}_{date.month}"
        base["time_index"] = last_time_index + horizon
        base["log_population"] = np.log(base["population"].astype(float))
        bases[horizon] = base
    return bases


def future_month_frame(base: pd.DataFrame, previous_state: pd.DataFrame, previous_statewide_deaths: float) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Attach lagged simulated counts and rates to a future county-month design frame.

    Args:
        See the function signature for input names and types.

    Returns:
        The dataframe, series, array, model object, or scalar described by the function name.
    """
    prev = previous_state[["county", "deaths", "deaths_per_capita"]].rename(columns={"deaths": "lag_deaths", "deaths_per_capita": "lag_rate"})
    frame = base.merge(prev, on="county", how="left", validate="one_to_one")
    frame["statewide_lag_deaths"] = float(previous_statewide_deaths)
    frame["log1p_lag_deaths"] = np.log1p(frame["lag_deaths"].fillna(0.0))
    frame["lag_rate_filled"] = frame["lag_rate"].fillna(0.0)
    frame["log1p_statewide_lag_deaths"] = np.log1p(frame["statewide_lag_deaths"])
    return frame


def simulate_negative_binomial(mu: np.ndarray, alpha: float, rng: np.random.Generator) -> np.ndarray:
    """Draw negative-binomial counts from fitted means and overdispersion.

    Args:
        See the function signature for input names and types.

    Returns:
        A NumPy array of simulated nonnegative integer counts.
    """
    mu = np.maximum(np.asarray(mu, dtype=float), 1e-12)
    alpha = max(float(alpha), 1e-12)
    n = 1.0 / alpha
    p = n / (n + mu)
    draws = rng.negative_binomial(n=n, p=p)
    if (draws < 0).any():
        raise ValueError("Negative-binomial simulation produced invalid negative counts.")
    return draws.astype(int)


def simulate_paths(train_df: pd.DataFrame, fit: CountModelFit, horizon_count: int, n_simulations: int, rng: np.random.Generator, observed_df_for_population: pd.DataFrame | None = None) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Simulate recursive county-month overdose count paths from a fitted count model.

    Args:
        See the function signature for input names and types.

    Returns:
        The dataframe, series, array, model object, or scalar described by the function name.
    """
    bases = future_base_for_dates(train_df, train_df["date"].max(), horizon_count, observed_df_for_population)
    last_observed = train_df.sort_values("date").groupby("county", sort=False).tail(1)[["county", "population", "deaths", "deaths_per_capita"]].copy()
    last_statewide_deaths = float(last_observed["deaths"].sum())
    rows = []
    for simulation in tqdm(range(n_simulations), desc=f"Simulating {horizon_count}-month county paths"):
        previous_state = last_observed.copy()
        previous_statewide = last_statewide_deaths
        for horizon in range(1, horizon_count + 1):
            frame = future_month_frame(bases[horizon], previous_state, previous_statewide)
            mu = np.maximum(np.asarray(fit.result.predict(frame, offset=frame["log_population"]), dtype=float), 1e-12)
            deaths = simulate_negative_binomial(mu, fit.alpha, rng)
            frame["simulation"] = simulation
            frame["horizon"] = horizon
            frame["mu"] = mu
            frame["deaths"] = deaths
            frame["deaths_per_capita"] = frame["deaths"] / frame["population"].astype(float)
            rows.append(frame[["simulation", "horizon", "date", "year", "month", "month_label", "county", "population", "mu", "deaths", "deaths_per_capita"]].copy())
            previous_state = frame[["county", "population", "deaths", "deaths_per_capita"]].copy()
            previous_statewide = float(previous_state["deaths"].sum())
    sims = pd.concat(rows, ignore_index=True)
    if (sims["deaths"] < 0).any():
        raise ValueError("Forecast simulations contain invalid negative counts.")
    return sims

# ---- County and topology simulation summaries: exceedance probabilities, statewide induced rates, induced H1 summaries, and direct H1 scalar forecasts ----

def summarize_county_simulations(sims: pd.DataFrame, hotspot_threshold: float) -> tuple[pd.DataFrame, pd.DataFrame]:
    """Summarize simulated county exceedance probabilities and exceeding-county counts.

    Args:
        See the function signature for input names and types.

    Returns:
        The dataframe, series, array, model object, or scalar described by the function name.
    """
    work = sims.copy()
    work["exceeds_hotspot_threshold"] = work["deaths_per_capita"] > hotspot_threshold
    county = (
        work.groupby(["county", "horizon", "date"], as_index=False)
        .agg(
            exceedance_probability=("exceeds_hotspot_threshold", "mean"),
            expected_deaths=("deaths", "mean"),
            median_deaths=("deaths", "median"),
            deaths_lower_50=("deaths", lambda x: float(np.quantile(x, 0.25))),
            deaths_upper_50=("deaths", lambda x: float(np.quantile(x, 0.75))),
            deaths_lower_80=("deaths", lambda x: float(np.quantile(x, 0.10))),
            deaths_upper_80=("deaths", lambda x: float(np.quantile(x, 0.90))),
            deaths_lower_95=("deaths", lambda x: float(np.quantile(x, 0.025))),
            deaths_upper_95=("deaths", lambda x: float(np.quantile(x, 0.975))),
            expected_rate=("deaths_per_capita", "mean"),
            median_rate=("deaths_per_capita", "median"),
            rate_lower_50=("deaths_per_capita", lambda x: float(np.quantile(x, 0.25))),
            rate_upper_50=("deaths_per_capita", lambda x: float(np.quantile(x, 0.75))),
            rate_lower_80=("deaths_per_capita", lambda x: float(np.quantile(x, 0.10))),
            rate_upper_80=("deaths_per_capita", lambda x: float(np.quantile(x, 0.90))),
            rate_lower_95=("deaths_per_capita", lambda x: float(np.quantile(x, 0.025))),
            rate_upper_95=("deaths_per_capita", lambda x: float(np.quantile(x, 0.975))),
        )
    )
    county["hotspot_threshold"] = hotspot_threshold
    count_dist = (
        work.groupby(["simulation", "horizon", "date"], as_index=False)
        .agg(n_exceeding_counties=("exceeds_hotspot_threshold", "sum"))
    )
    expected = (
        count_dist.groupby(["horizon", "date"], as_index=False)
        .agg(
            mean_exceeding_counties=("n_exceeding_counties", "mean"),
            median_exceeding_counties=("n_exceeding_counties", "median"),
            lower_80=("n_exceeding_counties", lambda x: float(np.quantile(x, 0.10))),
            upper_80=("n_exceeding_counties", lambda x: float(np.quantile(x, 0.90))),
            lower_95=("n_exceeding_counties", lambda x: float(np.quantile(x, 0.025))),
            upper_95=("n_exceeding_counties", lambda x: float(np.quantile(x, 0.975))),
            probability_at_least_one=("n_exceeding_counties", lambda x: float(np.mean(np.asarray(x) >= 1))),
            probability_at_least_5=("n_exceeding_counties", lambda x: float(np.mean(np.asarray(x) >= 5))),
            probability_at_least_10=("n_exceeding_counties", lambda x: float(np.mean(np.asarray(x) >= 10))),
        )
    )
    return county, expected


def summarize_statewide_from_county_sims(sims: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Summarize statewide rates induced by simulated county count paths.

    Args:
        See the function signature for input names and types.

    Returns:
        The dataframe, series, array, model object, or scalar described by the function name.
    """
    sim_state = (
        sims.groupby(["simulation", "horizon", "date"], as_index=False)
        .agg(statewide_deaths=("deaths", "sum"), statewide_population=("population", "sum"))
        .assign(statewide_rate=lambda x: x["statewide_deaths"] / x["statewide_population"])
    )
    return (
        sim_state.groupby(["horizon", "date"])["statewide_rate"]
        .apply(lambda x: interval_summary(x, "statewide_rate"))
        .unstack()
        .reset_index()
        .rename(columns={"statewide_rate_mean": "predicted_rate", "statewide_rate_median": "predicted_median_rate"})
    )


def summarize_h1_simulations(sim_h1: pd.DataFrame, sim_ranked_h1: pd.DataFrame, h1_threshold_value: float) -> tuple[pd.DataFrame, pd.DataFrame]:
    """Summarize model-induced H1 simulations by horizon and rank.
    This would be predicting future H1 if the predictions of future (county,month)
    overdose rates above was actually trustworthy.

    Args:
        See the function signature for input names and types.

    Returns:
        The dataframe, series, array, model object, or scalar described by the function name.
    """
    h1 = sim_h1.copy()
    h1["exceeds_h1_threshold"] = h1["max_h1_persistence"] > h1_threshold_value
    h1_summary = (
        h1.groupby(["horizon", "date"])["max_h1_persistence"]
        .apply(lambda x: interval_summary(x, "max_h1"))
        .unstack()
        .reset_index()
    )
    h1_extra = h1.groupby(["horizon", "date"], as_index=False).agg(
        mean_h1_point_count=("h1_point_count", "mean"),
        median_top_ranked_total_persistence=("top_ranked_total_persistence", "median"),
        prob_exceeds_h1_threshold=("exceeds_h1_threshold", "mean"),
        n_topology_simulations=("simulation", "nunique"),
    )
    h1_summary = h1_summary.merge(h1_extra, on=["horizon", "date"], how="left")
    ranked_summary = (
        sim_ranked_h1.groupby(["horizon", "date", "rank"])["persistence"]
        .apply(lambda x: interval_summary(x, "persistence"))
        .unstack()
        .reset_index()
    )
    return h1_summary, ranked_summary

```

The county-level negative-binomial model is not used for final forecasts below. We still keep a residual screen because the attempted county model is part of the forecasting story: it explains why the later sections focus on scalar statewide and scalar $H_1$ forecasting. This screen uses the held-out county forecast residuals created by the production chunk above, so the diagnostic is based on data not used to fit the model. We compute pooled lag-1 and lag-12 residual autocorrelation, countywise Ljung-Box failures after Benjamini-Hochberg adjustment, and monthly residual Moran's $I$. The last column, `passes_residual_screen`, is `True` only when the temporal and spatial residual checks are all mild enough that we would be willing to treat the remaining errors as approximately independent noise. In this section, all our models fail the residual tests for independence, but in later sections the pure time series models will pass the tests.

```{python}
#| label: county-nb-residual-screen-source
#| eval: false
from pathlib import Path
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from statsmodels.stats.diagnostic import acorr_ljungbox
from statsmodels.stats.multitest import multipletests

forecast_dir = Path.cwd().resolve()
if not (forecast_dir.parent / "data" / "county_drug_death.csv").exists():
    forecast_dir = forecast_dir / "forecasting files"
output_dir = forecast_dir / "forecasting_vineyards_outputs"
county_holdouts = pd.read_csv(output_dir / "county_forecast_holdouts_all.csv", parse_dates=["date"])

def _canonical_county_name(x):
    """Normalize county labels for the residual-screen adjacency file.

    Args:
        See the function signature for input names and types.

    Returns:
        The dataframe, series, array, model object, or scalar described by the function name.
    """
    return str(x).replace(" County, OH", "").replace(" County", "").strip().replace("Van Wert", "VanWert")

def _load_edges(adjacency_path):
    """Load county adjacency edges for Moran residual diagnostics.

    Args:
        See the function signature for input names and types.

    Returns:
        The dataframe, series, array, model object, or scalar described by the function name.
    """
    edges = set()
    counties = set()
    with open(adjacency_path, "r", encoding="utf-8") as handle:
        for line in handle:
            parts = [p.strip() for p in line.split() if p.strip()]
            if not parts:
                continue
            county = _canonical_county_name(parts[0])
            if county.lower() == "exterior":
                continue
            counties.add(county)
            for nbr in parts[1:]:
                nbr = _canonical_county_name(nbr)
                if nbr.lower() == "exterior":
                    continue
                counties.add(nbr)
                edges.add(tuple(sorted((county, nbr))))
    return sorted(counties), sorted(edges)

def _safe_corr(x, lag):
    """Compute a lagged correlation when enough nonconstant data are available.

    Args:
        See the function signature for input names and types.

    Returns:
        The dataframe, series, array, model object, or scalar described by the function name.
    """
    x = pd.Series(x).dropna().astype(float)
    if len(x) <= lag or x.std(ddof=1) <= 0:
        return np.nan
    return float(x.autocorr(lag=lag))

def _moran_i(values, counties, edges):
    """Compute Moran’s I for one vector of county residuals.

    Args:
        See the function signature for input names and types.

    Returns:
        The dataframe, series, array, model object, or scalar described by the function name.
    """
    v = pd.Series(values, index=counties).astype(float)
    x = v - v.mean()
    denom = float((x ** 2).sum())
    if denom <= 0 or not edges:
        return np.nan
    numer = sum(float(x[a] * x[b]) for a, b in edges if a in x.index and b in x.index)
    return float(len(counties) / len(edges) * numer / denom)

def _moran_permutation_p(values, counties, edges, rng, n_perm=199):
    """Compute a permutation p-value for Moran’s I.

    Args:
        See the function signature for input names and types.

    Returns:
        The dataframe, series, array, model object, or scalar described by the function name.
    """
    observed = _moran_i(values, counties, edges)
    if not np.isfinite(observed):
        return observed, np.nan
    arr = np.asarray(pd.Series(values, index=counties).astype(float), dtype=float)
    sims = []
    for _ in range(n_perm):
        sims.append(_moran_i(rng.permutation(arr), counties, edges))
    sims = np.asarray(sims, dtype=float)
    p = (1 + np.sum(np.abs(sims) >= abs(observed))) / (len(sims) + 1)
    return observed, float(p)

def build_county_residual_screen(county_holdouts, adjacency_path, output_path):
    """Build the county negative-binomial residual-screen summary table.

    Args:
        See the function signature for input names and types.

    Returns:
        The dataframe, series, array, model object, or scalar described by the function name.
    """
    counties, edges = _load_edges(adjacency_path)
    work = county_holdouts.copy()
    work["county"] = work["county"].map(_canonical_county_name)
    work["death_residual"] = work["observed_deaths"] - work["expected_deaths"]
    work["rate_residual"] = work["observed_rate"] - work["expected_rate"]
    rows = []
    rng = np.random.default_rng(20260622)
    for (experiment, train_end, holdout_year), group in work.groupby(["experiment", "train_end", "holdout_year"]):
        group = group.sort_values(["county", "date"])
        county_lb_p = []
        county_lag1 = []
        county_lag12 = []
        for county, cgroup in group.groupby("county"):
            resid = cgroup.sort_values("date")["death_residual"].astype(float)
            county_lag1.append(_safe_corr(resid, 1))
            county_lag12.append(_safe_corr(resid, 12))
            if resid.notna().sum() >= 8 and resid.std(ddof=1) > 0:
                lb = acorr_ljungbox(resid, lags=[min(3, max(1, len(resid) // 3))], return_df=True)
                county_lb_p.append(float(lb["lb_pvalue"].iloc[0]))
        if county_lb_p:
            reject_lb = multipletests(county_lb_p, alpha=0.05, method="fdr_bh")[0]
            prop_ljung_fail = float(np.mean(reject_lb))
            n_ljung_fail = int(np.sum(reject_lb))
        else:
            prop_ljung_fail = np.nan
            n_ljung_fail = 0
        moran_rows = []
        for date, mgroup in group.groupby("date"):
            residual_by_county = mgroup.set_index("county")["rate_residual"].reindex(counties).fillna(0.0)
            moran_i, moran_p = _moran_permutation_p(residual_by_county, counties, edges, rng)
            moran_rows.append({"date": date, "moran_i": moran_i, "pvalue": moran_p})
        moran_df = pd.DataFrame(moran_rows)
        if len(moran_df) and moran_df["pvalue"].notna().any():
            reject_moran = multipletests(moran_df["pvalue"].fillna(1.0), alpha=0.05, method="fdr_bh")[0]
            prop_moran_fail = float(np.mean(reject_moran))
            n_moran_fail = int(np.sum(reject_moran))
            median_moran = float(moran_df["moran_i"].median())
            mean_moran = float(moran_df["moran_i"].mean())
        else:
            prop_moran_fail = np.nan
            n_moran_fail = 0
            median_moran = np.nan
            mean_moran = np.nan
        pooled = group.sort_values(["county", "date"])["death_residual"]
        pooled_lag1 = _safe_corr(pooled, 1)
        pooled_lag12 = _safe_corr(pooled, 12)
        passes = (
            abs(pooled_lag1) < 0.10 if np.isfinite(pooled_lag1) else False
        ) and (
            abs(pooled_lag12) < 0.10 if np.isfinite(pooled_lag12) else False
        ) and (prop_ljung_fail <= 0.10 if np.isfinite(prop_ljung_fail) else False) and (prop_moran_fail <= 0.10 if np.isfinite(prop_moran_fail) else False)
        rows.append({
            "experiment": experiment,
            "train_end": train_end,
            "holdout_year": int(holdout_year),
            "pooled_lag1_residual_autocorrelation": pooled_lag1,
            "pooled_lag12_residual_autocorrelation": pooled_lag12,
            "county_lag1_residual_autocorrelation_median": float(np.nanmedian(county_lag1)),
            "county_lag12_residual_autocorrelation_median": float(np.nanmedian(county_lag12)),
            "n_counties_ljung_box_reject_bh_0_05": n_ljung_fail,
            "prop_counties_ljung_box_reject_bh_0_05": prop_ljung_fail,
            "mean_monthly_residual_morans_i": mean_moran,
            "median_monthly_residual_morans_i": median_moran,
            "n_months_residual_morans_i_reject_bh_0_05": n_moran_fail,
            "prop_months_residual_morans_i_reject_bh_0_05": prop_moran_fail,
            "passes_residual_screen": bool(passes),
        })
    screen = pd.DataFrame(rows)
    screen.to_csv(output_path, index=False)
    return screen

county_residual_screen = build_county_residual_screen(
    county_holdouts,
    forecast_dir.parent / "data" / "ohio_neighbors.txt",
    output_dir / "county_negative_binomial_residual_screen.csv",
)
county_residual_screen
```


In order for this model to produce trustworthy forecasts, it should leave no important residual spatiotemporal autocorrelation. We illustrate below how to check that, using the holdout data (the data not used in training the model). We pool model residuals within each holdout experiment and check whether the remaining errors look close enough to independent noise. The lag-1 residual autocorrelation is still large for each cutoff, so the residuals fail to be independent. The spatial Moran summaries are smaller, but the temporal dependence alone is enough so that we cannot forecast from this model.

```{python}
#| label: county-negative-binomial-residual-data
county_residual_screen = read_derived_csv("county_negative_binomial_residual_screen.csv")
```

```{python}
#| label: county-negative-binomial-residual-screen
county_residual_screen.drop(columns=[
    "county_lag12_residual_autocorrelation_median",
    "n_counties_ljung_box_reject_bh_0_05",
], errors="ignore")
```

Since we cannot forecast $D(c,t)$, we turn to forecasting statewide deaths per capita from its own past. This is easier, but it ignores county differences. Forecasting each of 88 county time series separately would ignore the spatiotemporal dependence documented in the other notebooks. Forecasting the max $H_1$ persistence, $M_t$, is a compromise: it reduces the full Ohio county surface to one time series, but the resulting forecasts still retain local information, rather than aggregating to the state level.

## An Overview of Time Series Forecasting

A time-series forecast uses observations through a cutoff date to predict values after that cutoff. The idea is to first fit a model that leaves random and independent residuals (with no further pattern), then continue the model forward. For example, if the model was $D_t = 0.9*D_{t-1} + \epsilon_t$ (where $\epsilon_t$ is the error term), then we could predict next month based on this month as $D_{t+1} = 0.9*D_t$ and we could predict the month after as $D_{t+2} = 0.9*\hat{D_{t+1}}$ where the hat notation means we use our prediction of $D_{t+1}$ since we would not know the true value yet when we made the prediction.

There are entire books on time series models and forecasting [11, 17]. The kinds of parametric models we are interested in here are SARIMA models meaning Seasonal AutoRegressive Integrated Moving Average models. We fit such a model in three steps. First we apply a differencing transformation if needed to make the time series stationary, e.g., shifting focus from $D_t$ to a new time series $\Delta D_t = D_t - D_{t-1}$ for first-order differencing, or $\Delta_{12} D_t = D_t - D_{t-12}$ for seasonal differencing (since 12 months ago might tell us something about the current month, like if it's winter or summer). Note that if you could predict these transformed variables, you could also use them for forecasting $D_{t+h}$ for any $h$. This is the "integrated" part of SARIMA. Let's use $Y_t$ to denote our (possibly transformed) response variable. Next, we fit autoregressive terms like $Y_t = \phi_1 Y_{t-1} + \phi_2 Y_{t-2} + \phi_{12} Y_{t-12}$, etc. We choose which lagged terms to include based on partial autocorrelation plots (PACF), and we find the best-fitting values of the coefficients using maximum likelihood estimation. Next, we fit the moving average terms like $\psi_1 \epsilon_{t-1} + \psi_2 \epsilon_{t-2} + \psi_{12} \epsilon_{t-12}$ which allow previous shock events to influence future values. Now the lags are chosen using autocorreation plots (ACF) and again the $\psi$-terms are estimated using maximum likelihood estimation. 

A fitted model is useful for forecasting only if the residuals look like random and independent noise. For SARIMA models we use the usual residual checks: the residual autocorrelation function should have no remaining pattern and the Ljung-Box test should fail to reject the null hypothesis of residual independence.

For example, we found that a SARIMA(0,1,3)x(0,0,3)[12] model fits well to the monthly overdose death time series $D_t$. That means we transform so $Y_t = \Delta D_t$ then we model 
$$Y_t = \psi_1 \epsilon_{t-1} +\psi_2 \epsilon_{t-2} +\psi_3 \epsilon_{t-3} +\psi_{12} \epsilon_{t-12} +\psi_{t-24} \epsilon_{t-24} +\psi_{t-36} \epsilon_{t-36} +\epsilon_t$$
Often, we have multiple good-fitting models (that leave independent residuals). In that case, we compare them using the Akaite Information Criteria (AIC). The model with the lowest AIC is preferred, as a theorem in statistics implies that model should also have the best predictive accuracy. For more details, see [17].

Note that forecast uncertainty grows with the time horizon: the model is not only uncertain about the next month, but also about the forecasted values that later months may depend on. We can make confidence intervals for forecasts using either bootstrapping or the normal distribution, if the residuals appear normal (visually and using standard statistical tests for normality, [17]).

## Our forecasts for Ohio statewide mortality and max $H_1$ persistence

The next non-executed source code shows how the scalar time-series diagnostics and residual-bootstrap forecast outputs are generated. They fit the SARIMAX residual-diagnostic models, run the nonparametric scalar holdouts, and save the selected forecast-band figures used below.

```{python}
#| label: scalar-time-series-forecast-source
#| eval: false

def sarima_statewide_forecast(train_state: pd.DataFrame, holdout_dates: list[pd.Timestamp]) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Fit and forecast the statewide-rate SARIMAX benchmark.

    Args:
        See the function signature for input names and types.

    Returns:
        The dataframe, series, array, model object, or scalar described by the function name.
    """
    y = train_state.sort_values("date").set_index("date")["statewide_rate"].asfreq("MS")
    rows = []
    try:
        model = SARIMAX(y, order=(1, 1, 1), seasonal_order=(1, 0, 1, 12), trend="c", enforce_stationarity=False, enforce_invertibility=False)
        with warnings.catch_warnings():
            warnings.simplefilter("ignore", ConvergenceWarning)
            result = model.fit(disp=False, maxiter=300)
        forecast = result.get_forecast(steps=len(holdout_dates))
        mean = np.asarray(forecast.predicted_mean, dtype=float)
        ci80 = np.asarray(forecast.conf_int(alpha=0.20), dtype=float)
        ci95 = np.asarray(forecast.conf_int(alpha=0.05), dtype=float)
        for i, date in enumerate(holdout_dates):
            rows.append({"model": "sarimax_statewide_rate", "horizon": i + 1, "date": date, "predicted_rate": float(mean[i]), "lower_80": float(ci80[i, 0]), "upper_80": float(ci80[i, 1]), "lower_95": float(ci95[i, 0]), "upper_95": float(ci95[i, 1]), "aic": float(result.aic), "bic": float(result.bic), "converged": True})
    except Exception as exc:
        for i, date in enumerate(holdout_dates):
            rows.append({"model": "sarimax_statewide_rate", "horizon": i + 1, "date": date, "predicted_rate": np.nan, "lower_80": np.nan, "upper_80": np.nan, "lower_95": np.nan, "upper_95": np.nan, "error": repr(exc), "converged": False})
    return pd.DataFrame(rows)
```

We also provide the code below to do a SARIMA-style forecast of max $H_1$ persistence, discussed in the next section.
```{python}
#| label: direct-h1-scalar-forecast-source
#| eval: false
def direct_h1_scalar_forecast(train_h1: pd.DataFrame, holdout_dates: list[pd.Timestamp], h1_threshold_value: float) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Forecast maximum H1 persistence directly as a scalar time series.

    Args:
        See the function signature for input names and types.

    Returns:
        The dataframe, series, array, model object, or scalar described by the function name.
    """
    train = train_h1.sort_values("date").set_index("date")["max_h1_persistence"].asfreq("MS")
    rows = []
    y = train.to_numpy(dtype=float)
    step_sd = float(np.std(np.diff(y), ddof=1)) if len(y) > 2 else float(np.std(y, ddof=1))
    recent_mean = float(train.tail(12).mean())
    last_value = float(train.iloc[-1])
    for horizon, date in enumerate(holdout_dates, start=1):
        for model, mean in [("h1_recent_12_month_average", recent_mean), ("h1_random_walk", last_value)]:
            se = step_sd * math.sqrt(horizon)
            rows.append({"model": model, "horizon": horizon, "date": date, "forecast_mean": mean, "lower_80": mean - 1.2816 * se, "upper_80": mean + 1.2816 * se, "lower_95": mean - 1.96 * se, "upper_95": mean + 1.96 * se, "prob_exceeds_h1_threshold": normal_tail_probability(h1_threshold_value, mean, se)})
    try:
        model = SARIMAX(train, order=(1, 0, 0), seasonal_order=(1, 0, 0, 12), trend="c", enforce_stationarity=False, enforce_invertibility=False)
        with warnings.catch_warnings():
            warnings.simplefilter("ignore", ConvergenceWarning)
            result = model.fit(disp=False, maxiter=300)
        forecast = result.get_forecast(steps=len(holdout_dates))
        mean = np.asarray(forecast.predicted_mean, dtype=float)
        ci80 = np.asarray(forecast.conf_int(alpha=0.20), dtype=float)
        ci95 = np.asarray(forecast.conf_int(alpha=0.05), dtype=float)
        se = np.asarray(forecast.se_mean, dtype=float)
        for i, date in enumerate(holdout_dates):
            rows.append({"model": "h1_sarimax_scalar", "horizon": i + 1, "date": date, "forecast_mean": float(mean[i]), "lower_80": float(ci80[i, 0]), "upper_80": float(ci80[i, 1]), "lower_95": float(ci95[i, 0]), "upper_95": float(ci95[i, 1]), "prob_exceeds_h1_threshold": normal_tail_probability(h1_threshold_value, float(mean[i]), float(se[i])), "aic": float(result.aic), "bic": float(result.bic)})
    except Exception as exc:
        for i, date in enumerate(holdout_dates):
            rows.append({"model": "h1_sarimax_scalar", "horizon": i + 1, "date": date, "forecast_mean": np.nan, "lower_80": np.nan, "upper_80": np.nan, "lower_95": np.nan, "upper_95": np.nan, "prob_exceeds_h1_threshold": np.nan, "error": repr(exc)})
    return pd.DataFrame(rows)

```

The next code block carries out residual diagnostics: checking residual ACF for independence, normality plots, and summary tests.

```{python}
#| label: scalar-residual-diagnostic-figure-source
#| eval: false

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.5, 6))
ax.scatter(statewide_predictions["statewide_rate"], statewide_predictions["predicted_rate"], s=18, alpha=0.6, color="#457b9d")
lim = np.nanmax([statewide_predictions["statewide_rate"].max(), statewide_predictions["predicted_rate"].max()]) * 1.05
ax.plot([0, lim], [0, lim], color="#333333", ls="--", lw=1)
ax.set_xlim(0, lim)
ax.set_ylim(0, lim)
ax.set_title("Observed versus predicted statewide rates in completed-year holdouts")
ax.set_xlabel("Observed deaths per capita")
ax.set_ylabel("Predicted deaths per capita")
fig.tight_layout()
for ext in ["png", "pdf"]:
    fig.savefig(FIGURE_DIR / f"statewide_observed_vs_predicted_cv.{ext}", dpi=180 if ext == "png" else None, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.5, 6))
ax.scatter(county_predictions["observed_rate"], county_predictions["expected_rate"], s=11, alpha=0.35, color="#264653")
lim = max(county_predictions["observed_rate"].max(), county_predictions["expected_rate"].max()) * 1.05
ax.plot([0, lim], [0, lim], color="#a23b72", ls="--", lw=1)
ax.set_xlim(0, lim)
ax.set_ylim(0, lim)
ax.set_title("Observed versus predicted county deaths per capita")
ax.set_xlabel("Observed deaths per capita")
ax.set_ylabel("Predicted mean deaths per capita")
fig.tight_layout()
for ext in ["png", "pdf"]:
    fig.savefig(FIGURE_DIR / f"county_observed_vs_predicted_cv.{ext}", dpi=180 if ext == "png" else None, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 5))
for year, group in exceedance_calibration.groupby("holdout_year"):
    group = group.dropna(subset=["mean_predicted_probability", "observed_frequency"]).sort_values("mean_predicted_probability")
    ax.plot(group["mean_predicted_probability"], group["observed_frequency"], marker="o", lw=1.5, label=str(year))
ax.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], color="#333333", ls="--", lw=1)
ax.set_xlim(0, 1)
ax.set_ylim(0, 1)
ax.set_title("Hotspot exceedance calibration in completed-year holdouts")
ax.set_xlabel("Mean predicted exceedance probability")
ax.set_ylabel("Observed exceedance frequency")
ax.legend(title="Holdout year")
fig.tight_layout()
for ext in ["png", "pdf"]:
    fig.savefig(FIGURE_DIR / f"exceedance_calibration_cv.{ext}", dpi=180 if ext == "png" else None, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()

# ---- Residual-diagnostic execution: fit scalar SARIMAX models and save diagnostic plots/tables ----

for year in [2022, 2023]:
    sub = expected_counts_holdout[expected_counts_holdout["holdout_year"].eq(year)].sort_values("date")
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 4.5))
    ax.plot(sub["date"], sub["median_exceeding_counties"], marker="o", color="#264653", label="Median")
    ax.fill_between(sub["date"], sub["lower_80"], sub["upper_80"], color="#e9c46a", alpha=0.35, label="80% interval")
    ax.fill_between(sub["date"], sub["lower_95"], sub["upper_95"], color="#f4a261", alpha=0.18, label="95% interval")
    observed_counts = county_predictions[county_predictions["holdout_year"].eq(year)].groupby("date")["observed_exceeds_hotspot_threshold"].sum().reset_index()
    ax.plot(observed_counts["date"], observed_counts["observed_exceeds_hotspot_threshold"], marker="s", color="#a23b72", label="Observed")
    ax.set_title(f"Number of counties above the hotspot threshold, {year} holdout")
    ax.set_xlabel("Held-out month")
    ax.set_ylabel("County count")
    ax.legend()
    fig.tight_layout()
    for ext in ["png", "pdf"]:
        fig.savefig(FIGURE_DIR / f"expected_exceeding_counties_holdout_{year}.{ext}", dpi=180 if ext == "png" else None, bbox_inches="tight")
    plt.show()

for year in [2022, 2023]:
    sub = h1_predictions[h1_predictions["holdout_year"].eq(year)].sort_values("date")
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 4.6))
    ax.plot(sub["date"], sub["observed_max_h1_persistence"], marker="o", color="#111111", label="Observed")
    ax.plot(sub["date"], sub["max_h1_median"], marker="o", color="#2a9d8f", label="Forecast median")
    ax.fill_between(sub["date"], sub["max_h1_lower_80"], sub["max_h1_upper_80"], color="#2a9d8f", alpha=0.18, label="80% interval")
    ax.fill_between(sub["date"], sub["max_h1_lower_95"], sub["max_h1_upper_95"], color="#2a9d8f", alpha=0.09, label="95% interval")
    ax.axhline(h1_threshold, color="#a23b72", ls=":", lw=1.1, label="Reference H1 threshold")
    ax.set_title(f"Model-induced max H1 forecast, {year} holdout")
    ax.set_xlabel("Held-out month")
    ax.set_ylabel("Max finite H1 persistence")
    ax.legend(fontsize=8)
    fig.tight_layout()
    for ext in ["png", "pdf"]:
        fig.savefig(FIGURE_DIR / f"h1_max_persistence_holdout_{year}_forecast_band.{ext}", dpi=180 if ext == "png" else None, bbox_inches="tight")
    plt.show()

# ---- Nonparametric scalar forecasts: mean, naive, drift, seasonal naive, recent same-month, and STL/LOESS residual bootstrap ----

def target_series_for_diagnostics(target: str) -> pd.Series:
    """Load a scalar series for SARIMAX residual diagnostics.

    Args:
        See the function signature for input names and types.

    Returns:
        The dataframe, series, array, model object, or scalar described by the function name.
    """
    if target == "statewide_rate":
        return statewide_series(completed_df).set_index("date")["statewide_rate"].asfreq("MS")
    if target == "max_h1_persistence":
        return observed_h1.set_index("date")["max_h1_persistence"].asfreq("MS")
    raise ValueError(target)


def fit_sarimax_for_target(train_series: pd.Series, target: str):
    """Fit the SARIMAX model used for scalar residual diagnostics.

    Args:
        See the function signature for input names and types.

    Returns:
        The dataframe, series, array, model object, or scalar described by the function name.
    """
    if target == "statewide_rate":
        model = SARIMAX(
            train_series,
            order=(1, 1, 1),
            seasonal_order=(1, 0, 1, 12),
            trend="c",
            enforce_stationarity=False,
            enforce_invertibility=False,
        )
    elif target == "max_h1_persistence":
        model = SARIMAX(
            train_series,
            order=(1, 0, 0),
            seasonal_order=(1, 0, 0, 12),
            trend="c",
            enforce_stationarity=False,
            enforce_invertibility=False,
        )
    else:
        raise ValueError(target)
    with warnings.catch_warnings():
        warnings.simplefilter("ignore", ConvergenceWarning)
        return model.fit(disp=False, maxiter=300)


def summarize_residual_normality(residuals: pd.Series, target: str, train_end: pd.Timestamp) -> dict[str, Any]:
    """Summarize scalar SARIMAX residual normality and shape diagnostics.

    Args:
```
